coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots as a superior building nanomaterial for enhancing the mechanical properties of cement-based composites
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Haojie
Qian, Shanshan
Liu, Xiao
Gao, Ruijun
Wang, Ziming
Zheng, Chunyang
Zhang, Zhiyang
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and its derivatives can act as a significant building nanomaterial for ameliorating the various properties of cement-based composites, but their poor water solubility lead to unevenly dispersed in the cement pore solution, and limits their effective application in cement-based materials. Moreover, the high price of these nanomaterials restricts the practical use in industry. Herein, in this study, the carbon dots (CDs), which was prepared by the improved microwave pyrolysis method, is demonstrated to own the superior performance in the enhancement of the cement-based composites mechanical properties for the first time. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and related structural characterizations are performed to prove the successful synthesis of CDs, and the compressive strength of cement-based composites increased by 16.8% after curing 28 days at the 0.08 wt% CDs dosage in comparison to the blank samples. Through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and mercury intrusion porosimeter (MIP) and XRD, it is proved that: i) the small nanometer size and good water solubility of carbon dots promote them easily filling into the pores of cement as a result of increasing its compactness; ii) the carbon dots act as much more nucleation sites to make the hydration products superimposed in a more dense room; iii) the surface of carbon dots possess many hydrophilic groups, including carboxyl and amide, these groups produce a strong adsorption on the cement particles and then affect the formation of hydration products. These joint effects effectively enhance the compressive mechanical strength for cement-based composites. Besides, the simple preparation of CDs with a low price is suitable for the industrial application. The CDs as the superior alternative nanomaterial for heightening the properties of cement-based composites show a great potential, and is significant for the exploitation of building nanomaterial with outstanding performances for cement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cement-based composites
Mechanical strength
Nano-size

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Workability and mechanical properties of concrete
Preparation and characteristics of the CDs
Application performances of CDs in concrete
Influence of CDs on slump and slump flow of concrete
Effect of CDs on the compressive strength of concrete
Exploration of the effect of adding CDs on the development of microstructure of cement
Influence of CDs on pore structure of concrete
The mechanism of CDs on the mechanical properties of cement-based composites

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support from the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund ( CX(20)3079 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22078136 ), the Guangxi Science and Technology Program ( AB20159011 ), the Cooperative Research Project of BJUT-NTUT (NTUT-BJUT-110-04), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, CHD ( 300102311502 ) and the authors would like to thank zhang qian from Shi yan jia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the TG analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical images of liquid CDs and solid CDs prepared by the process of improved microwave pyrolysis approach.
(a) HR-TEM results of CDs; (b) Size distribution of CDs; (c) X-ray diffraction of CDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
The XPS results of (a) full scan; (b) C1s; (c) N1s and (d) O1s.

(a) Optical images of different content CDs in cement pore solution,(b) Compressive strength of concrete with various dosages after curing for 3 d, 7 d and 28 d, (c) Ratio of increasing strength of concrete with various dosages after curing for 3 d, 7 d and 28 d.
XRD patterns at different ages: (a) curing for 3 days, (b) curing for 7 days, (c) curing for 28 days.
(a) Thermogravimetric analysis and (b) CH content of blank and CDs at different curing ages, 3d, 7d and 28d, respectively.
SEM images of cement with or without CDs, (a) blank for 3 d, (b) CDs for 3 d, (c) blank for 7 d, (d) CDs for 7 d, (e) blank for 28 d, (f) CDs for 28 d.
(a) Log differential intrusion and (b) Cumulative pore area of blank and CDs after 28 days of hydration (dosage of CDs with is 0.04%).
Schematic diagram of the effect of CDs on cement hydration with enhancing its mechanical strength.
Chemical compositions of prepared CDs through elemental analysis.
Slump performance of carbon dots in the concrete.
Pore volume characteristics of blank and CDs obtained through MIP.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots as a superior building nanomaterial for enhancing the mechanical properties of cement-based composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu ARIT New Materials Co., Ltd, Nanjing, 211505, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu ARIT New Materials Co., Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu ARIT New Materials Co., Ltd, Nanjing, 211505, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agricultural Resources and Environment, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing, 210014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agricultural Resources and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agricultural Resources and Environment, Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Nanjing, 210014, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials and Manufacturing, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials and Manufacturing, Key Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, 100124, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: China Building Materials Academy, Beijing, 100024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: China Building Materials Academy, Beijing, 100024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and its derivatives can act as a significant building nanomaterial for ameliorating the various properties of cement-based composites, but their poor water solubility lead to unevenly dispersed in the cement pore solution, and limits their effective application in cement-based materials. Moreover, the high price of these nanomaterials restricts the practical use in industry. Herein, in this study, the carbon dots (CDs), which was prepared by the improved microwave pyrolysis method, is demonstrated to own the superior performance in the enhancement of the cement-based composites mechanical properties for the first time. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and related structural characterizations are performed to prove the successful synthesis of CDs, and the compressive strength of cement-based composites increased by 16.8% after curing 28 days at the 0.08 wt% CDs dosage in comparison to the blank samples. Through thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and mercury intrusion porosimeter (MIP) and XRD, it is proved that: i) the small nanometer size and good water solubility of carbon dots promote them easily filling into the pores of cement as a result of increasing its compactness; ii) the carbon dots act as much more nucleation sites to make the hydration products superimposed in a more dense room; iii) the surface of carbon dots possess many hydrophilic groups, including carboxyl and amide, these groups produce a strong adsorption on the cement particles and then affect the formation of hydration products. These joint effects effectively enhance the compressive mechanical strength for cement-based composites. Besides, the simple preparation of CDs with a low price is suitable for the industrial application. The CDs as the superior alternative nanomaterial for heightening the properties of cement-based composites show a great potential, and is significant for the exploitation of building nanomaterial with outstanding performances for cement.

Image 1
.The CDs is firstly used as a superior building nanomaterial in the concrete field. The joint effects of nanometer size and hydrophilic groups in CDs, which is revealed by various characterizations, is responsibly to the superior mechanical properties of cement-based composites.

ce:section.ce:para: The cement-based building materials are employed for a long time in construction engineering field. The aggregate particles are firmly bonded into a whole owning to the materials act as an adhesive that glues all materials together [1]. However, the inherent large porosity and quasi-brittle nature of cement-based materials makes crack propagation after loading, thus affecting their mechanical properties and limited their applications [1,2]. In order to solve these thorny problems, traditional fiber reinforcements, for example, glass, carbon and polymer are often added. However, crack propagation can only be restricted at macro level, not at nano level, defects in the cement structure will still affect its strength [3–5]. In recent years, there is an increasing attention to the use of nanostructured material to control the rheological and mechanical properties for cements through its various functions such as size or morphology effect [6].

In particular, the application of different dimensional nanoparticles, nanowires or nanosheets have captured the attention of researchers in the field of construction [7]. The improvement of rheology by using those nanomaterials is primarily thanks to: i) the nanomaterials own the high adsorption for the free water attributing to their high specific surface area and nano scale size, ii) the interconnection between the binder particles such as cement, sand and stone are further strengthened in the presence of nanomaterials, iii) strengthen the density of the particle bulk, iv) increased nucleation sites due to nucleation and templating, thus affecting the cement hydration [8,9]. Among them, using nanomaterials ameliorates the mechanical performance of the composites, which is mainly caused by the enhancement in nucleation sites and packing density. Nanoparticles such as SiO2 or TiO2 act as heterogeneous nuclei of the cement slurry, making the microstructure denser through reducing the porosity [10–12]. Herein, these nanomaterials are commonly used as the reinforcing materials for enhancing the mechanical performance of cement. Especially, carbon nanomaterials, such as graphene and its derivatives, are most used for ameliorating the cement properties and also can exist stably in the water solution [13,14], but their water-insoluble characteristics lead to unevenly dispersion in the cement pore solution, which limits their effective application in cement-based materials [15–17]. Many researchers improve their dispersion properties by physical and chemical modification, but the process is complicated and is not conducive to large-scale applications [18–20]. Moreover, the high price of graphene oxide and carbon nanotubes highly restrict their practical application in industry.

The novel nanomaterial of carbon dots (defined as CDs) has attracted much attention because of its outstanding chemical stability, long-term durability, corrosion resistance, low cost and non-toxicity, also possesses adjustable luminescent properties [21,22]. At present, CDs is widespread applied in different fields, including catalysis, electrochemistry and photovoltaics [23,24]. Nevertheless, there are few reports on its application in the concrete field in previous reported works.

Herein, in this study, we report the use of CDs in cement-based composites for the first time, and clarify the mechanisms by which CDs affect the workability, compressive strength and microstructure of cement-based materials. The CDs prepared through the improved microwave pyrolysis method possess many hydrophilic groups and average ∼22.60 nm size. The applications of CDs in concrete including the slump, slump flow and compressive strength were tested in detail. The results show that adding of CDs had no adverse effect on the slump, and the compressive strength is improved. It is demonstrated that the superior performance for heightening the cement-based composites compressive strength is due to the joint effects of nano-size and surface functional groups in CDs. In detail, the results of TGA, XRD and SEM show that the content of C–S–H and Ca(OH)2 in the CDs composites is much higher than that in the blank sample, which affect the nucleation process and morphology growth of hydration products. Besides, the MIP test indicates that the addition of CDs makes the concrete material more compact in structure, more refined in pore size, lower in the porosity and more excellent in macroscopic mechanical properties. This work sheds light on the potentiality of the CDs as a novel alternative nanomaterial for heightening the performance of cement-based material, and is significant for developing building nanomaterial with outstanding performance of cement.

The experimental reagents in this work mainly contain: citric Acid (≥99.99%, Macklin), urea (≥99.00%, Chinese Medicine Reagent Co., Ltd), deionized H2O. In this work, P.I42.5 cement was purchased from China Building Materials Research Institute Co., Ltd. Table 1 shows the chemical compositions of the cement. The concrete mix proportion is given in Table 2 . The fineness modulus of medium sand is 2.7, and the continuous grade of gravel is 5–20 mm. The superplasticizer M61 is supplied by Jiangsu ARIT New Materials Co., Ltd from China.

1.50 g citric acid and 0.50 g urea were simultaneously added into the 10.0 mL deionized water with continuous stirring, followed by forming a clear and transparent solution. The solution was then performed to the treatment by microwave at the power of 450 W for 120 s in a domestic oven. After that, the aqueous solution of dark brown carbon quantum dots was obtained. The solid carbon quantum dots were prepared by the freezing drying method for 96 h in an ultra-high vacuum.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) measurement was tested on an instrument of Bruker D8 Advance, the scanning angle range is in 5–90° with the scanning speed of 10 °C/min. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed at the Nicolet iS10 in the spectral range of 400–4000 cm−1. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) results for the CDs was gained at JEM-2800 UHR. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiment were measured in a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, mainly testing three elements C, O, N. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was conducted at a NETZSCHSTA 449 °C by a 4 °C/min heating rate from RT to 800 °C by an alumina crucible. The chemical composition of prepared CDs is tested by the elemental analysis of elementar instrument (vario macro cube). The chemical composition of prepared CDs was tested by the elemental analysis of elementar instrument (vario macro cube). The freezing drying was performed on Alpha 2-4LSCbasic in an ultra-high vacuum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Workability and mechanical properties of concrete
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the concrete is prepared by mixing of cement, water, fine and coarse aggregate, superplasticizer and CDs with different contents in part of the mixing water according to the mixing proportion in Table 2. According to GB/T50080-2016 standard [25], the slump and slump flow rate of the concrete with different contents of CDs at initial and 1 h were evaluated by slump test. Then, to assess the effect of CDs adding on the compression strength properties of cement paste, the tests of compression for cement were carried out through the specimens with the size of 100 mm × 100 mm × 100 mm. The samples were cured at the different ages of 3 days, 7 days and 28 days by following the GB/T50081-2019 standard [26]. Compression strength test were measured by YAW-3000 automatic pressure testing machine produced by Sino-British joint venture Zhejiang Jingyuan Machinery Equipment Co., Ltd.

The cement-based composites owning various microstructure were analyzed with the measurements of XRD. For XRD, six specimens of Blank, CDs-0.02, CDs-0.04, CDs-0.06, CDs-0.08 and CDs-0.10 were prepared with CDs/cement mass ratio of 0%, 0.02%, 0.04%, 0.06%, 0.08% and 0.10%, respectively. The water/cement ratio for all specimens was kept at 0.29. The cement pastes were cured for different ages of 3 d, 7 d and 28 d, respectively, and then ground into powder by passing through 45 μm mesh sieves. The ethanol solution was used to prevent the hydration for 3 days, and then performed by drying for additional 3 days at 38 °C, and seal samples for characterization. XRD scans of various samples were recorded on Bruker D8 Advance. The scanning angle range of XRD is in the range of 5–60° with a Cu-Kα radiation source, and the scanning speed is 10 °C/min.

For TGA, the water/cement ratio for all specimens was also kept at 0.29. Six specimens were prepared with CDs/cement mass ratio of 0.04% for different cured time of 3 d, 7 d and 28 d, respectively. The method of drying and grinding into powder after termination of hydration is the same as that of XRD sample preparation. TG was measured on the instrument of NETZSCHSTA 449F3 under nitrogen flow. The temperature range of the test is 30–800 °C with the heating rate of 20 °C/min. The Ca(OH)2 (CH)content was measured by the TG. According to the TGA curves, the main weight loss from 400 to 500 °C is attributed to the Ca(OH)2 dehydration [27].
C H
/
% = W
L CH
×
MW CH
/
MW
H 20

In the above formula: CH represents the content of calcium hydroxide, %; WLCH is equivalent to the weight loss percentage of CH, %; MWCH and MWH2O represent the molecular weights of calcium hydroxide and H2O, respectively.

After curing for 3 d, 7 d and 28 d, the compression strength tests of concrete. By remaining the percentage content of CDs dosage is 0.04% of the cementitious material, SEM were used to explore the influence on the internal structure and porosity for concrete, and also the blank sample was as a reference. The SEM samples were crushed to 5 mm2 particles from concrete test block and soaked in alcohol solution to prevent its hydration. Finally, put them in an oven dry at 100 °C for 12 h to ensure completely dry. After that, scanning electron microscopy was carried out by Hitachi SU8010 from Japan for latter microstructure and morphology characterization of hydrated concrete samples.

After the 28d compression strength tests of concrete with sample of CDs dosage is 0.04% and blank sample. The samples were crushed to 15 mm × 15mm × 15 mm particles and soaked in alcohol solution to prevent its hydration, and at last dried for 12 h at 100 °C. Mercury intrusion porosimeter (MIP, Auto pore V9620) was performed by measuring the pore structure of concrete. Sample are weighed accurately in the interval of 2–3 g and the pressure range is 0.5–60000 psi.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characteristics of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs was synthesized through the improved microwave irradiation method under 450 W for 120 s through the mixing of aqueous solution of urea and citric acid, and the process is shown in Fig. 1 . The optical images of the liquid or solid CDs are also inserted in Fig. 1. During the process of microwave at high temperature, the urea and citric acid quickly dehydrates to form CDs. As can be easily seen from the optical images of CDs, the CDs exhibits a good dispersion in water and the solid CDs was prepared through the freeze drying in an ultra-high vacuum, which is taupe brown powder.

The TEM images are illustrated in Fig. 2 a, clearly shows that the prepared CDs are uniformly distributed in the water without the occurrence of agglomeration and own a mean size of 22.60 nm, which is given in Fig. 2b. The XRD spectrum of CDs in Fig. 2c displayed two peaks located at the position of 18.3° and 27.8°, suggesting the existing of graphite carbon with amorphous nature [28]. All peaks of FT-IR spectrum are illustrated in Fig. 2d, the broad peak of 3450 cm−1 and a shoulder peak of 3183 cm−1 are owing to the vibration from the O–H and N–H groups on CDs, respectively. The high stretching peak at 1701 cm−1 and the weaker peak of 1397 cm−1 are the CO group and CN group, respectively. Besides above peaks, the stretching peaks of C–O, CC and C–H groups are also exhibited in Fig. 2d. The results of the FT-IR data points out the existence of various hydrophilic groups on the CDs [29].

The surfaces properties for the CDs were further studied by XPS method, the results are shown in Fig. 3 . For the total element spectrum in Fig. 3a, it is clearly seen the existing of the elements of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, which is in consistent with the results of elemental analysis. As for the C spectrum in Fig. 3b, there exhibits three main binding energy locations. The peak at 284.3 eV is due to the CC, and the peaks at 286.2 eV and 288.1 eV belong to C–O or C–C, and CN or CO, respectively [30]. The N spectrum is shown in Fig. 3c, the peak at 399.5 eV comes from the pyrrolic N, and the peak at 401.2 eV belongs to N–H group. The O1s peak at 531.5 eV shown in Fig. 3d is the CO group, and the weaker peak at 532.9 eV is the C–O signal. The CDs were also performed through the elemental analysis, and the results shown in Table 3 , gives the C = 38.20%, H = 4.30%, O = 45.22%, and N = 12.28%, indicating the co-existing of these elements, which is in accordance with the XPS results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application performances of CDs in concrete
Influence of CDs on slump and slump flow of concrete
ce:section.ce:para: The application performances in concrete of carbon dots were tested, the chemical components of used cement are presented in Table 1 and the mix proportion of concrete including water-cement ratio is illustrated in Table 2. The mass ratio of CDs to cement are 0.00%, 0.02%, 0.04%, 0.06% 0.08% and 0.10%, respectively. Relevant studies [31–33] have shown that the addition of nanomaterial graphene oxide will have a negative effect on the slump, and the slump value decreases with the increase of the amount of graphene oxide, which affects its use to a certain extent. The experimental results of CDs on the slump and slump loss of the tested concrete are shown in Table 4 . Unlike graphene oxide, the use of CDs produces no adverse influence on the slump for concrete, fresh concrete doped with CDs showed high initial slump and low slump loss at different dosages. Due to the abundant hydrophilic groups in carbon dots, such as carboxyl and amide groups. And the dissociation of hydrogen atoms into protons produces acidity, enhancing the polarity of the –NH– bond, and Ca2+ forms ligands with nitrogen of amide structure [34]. Therefore, the carboxyl and amide groups of carbon dots will affect the concentration of Ca2+ in the cement pore solution to a certain extent, thus reducing the adverse effects on cement slump caused by large specific surface area and strong water absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CDs on the compressive strength of concrete
ce:section.ce:para: To study the influence of CDs on the mechanical performances of concrete, the compressive strengths were tested. Related studies [1,2,14,35] have found that GO is difficult to disperse uniformly in cement-based materials, so we tested the dispersion of CDs in cement pore solution. The photo images of cement pore solution using different CDs content are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The reference composition of the cement pore solution [36] is as follows: 4.757 g/L K2SO4, 7.12 g/L KOH, 6.959 g/L Na2SO4 and 1.72 g/L CaSO4·2H2O. The CDs are evenly distributed in the cement pore solution without precipitation for a long time, which can be clearly seen from Fig. 4a.

Fig. 4b shows the effect of different content of CDs on the compressive strength of concrete. It clearly explain the addition of CDs increases the compressive strength of the hardened concretes at 3, 7 and 28 days. With the increase of the content of CDs, the compressive strengths at various cured ages are gradually increased, but when the content is too high, the strength improvement effect will be weak. Compared to the controlled samples quantitatively, the compressive strength increased by 8.6%, 17.42% and 16.8%, respectively, after curing at 3,7 and 28 days at the content of the 0.08 wt% of the cementitious material. The above results can be seen from Fig. 4c. As reported by Yu Shang [32] et al., when the dosage of GO was 0.04%, its 28d compressive strength is increased by 15.1%, while the dosage was increased to 0.06%, the 28d compressive strength improvement ratio is reduced to 12.4%, that is to say, its maximum compressive strength improvement ratio is 15.1%. However, the 28d compressive strength with the addition of CDs increased by 16.8%, the ratio of increasing compressive strength is also higher than the study by Li Xiangyu [33]. There are also related reports [37,38] that the compressive strength of 28d increased by 30% or even higher by adding GO, but their price was relatively expensive, the preparation process was more complicated, and their dispersibility in cement-based materials was also poor than CDs. The high content of functional groups in CDs are beneficial to chemically bond with cement hydrates, and the small nano size of CDs promote its filling into the pore of the gap between cement. Those comprehensive effects lead to the enhancement of the compressive strength of cement-based composites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the effect of adding CDs on the development of microstructure of cement

ce:section.ce:para: The specimens added with various amounts of CDs are tested after curing for 3d, 7d and 28d days, the content of CDs is 0.02%–0.1% of the cement quality, named as CDs-0.02, CDs-0.04, CDs-0.06, CDs-0.08 and CDs-0.1, respectively, which the XRD patterns can be seen in Fig. 5a∼c. The detected phases are the typical cement hydrates such as AFt, CH and C–S–H. With the increase of the content of CDs, the peak intensities of CH and C–S–H gradually increase, it shows that with the continuous hydration of cement, cement clinker minerals are gradually hydrated to form other hydration products, which is beneficial to increase the production rate and quantity of C–S–H and CH hydration products. Therefore, the mechanical properties of the concrete are improved. But when the content is too high, the cement hydration may be slowed down due to the retarding effect, which is consistent with the compression results. CH corresponds to the peaks at 18.2°, 34.2°, 47.2° and 50.9° in the XRD patterns and the peaks height are related to the amount of the CH. The strength of the CH phase increased with the amount of CDs in the composites. Since XRD cannot be quantitatively analyzed, TG was used to analyze the amount of CH produced at different ages. The morphology of C–S–H by adding CDs were observed by SEM and will be explained below.

Fig. 6 a shows the TG results for the specimens after curing for 3d, 7d and 28 days when the content of CDs is 0.04%. The mass loss from TGA can be calculated from the content of CH, which reflects the different degrees of hydration of cement composites [39,40], correspondingly.

The CH content as a function of CDs dosages and curing time (3, 7 and 28 days) is calculated in Fig. 6b. From the data of CH content data at 3, 7 and 28 curing ages, the CH content of the carbon dot composites is greater than that of blank samples, which indicated that the hydration degree of the composites added with CDs is higher. Due to the rich functional groups of CDs, the nucleation process and morphology growth of hydration products are affected. Furthermore, the excellent hydrophilicity and large specific surface area of carbon dots allow it to grow on its surface. CDs with larger specific surface area will absorb more water molecules in the early stage of cement hydration, and these water molecules can be further released into free water at the later stage of cement hydration, which is helpful for the further progress of cement hydration, thereby further increasing the CH content at 28 d of hydration. TG analysis further verified the hydrated phase results determined by XRD.

To explore the specific impact of CDs nanoparticles on the microstructure of cement, thus SEM investigation was conducted to survey the fracture surface of complex binder systems [35,41,42]. The dosage of 0.04 wt% and the hydration time of 3d, 7d and 28d, respectively were employed, which result are shown in Fig. 7 .

Fig. 7a and b shows the results of the specimens at 3 d. The blank sample has large cracks locally, and it can be seen that the oxygen-containing hydrophilic groups in CDs result in the nucleation of hydration products, which can also attributes to the formation of chemical bonds between these groups and the hydration products such as C–S–H or CH. CDs are spherical shape and have a large specific surface area, which facilitates the hydration process to form C–S–H or CH species, and these hydration products further penetrate into voids and crack of cement as a result of filling these defect to enhance its mechanical strength.

Fig. 7c and d shows the specimens cured for 7 d. As can be seen from the blank sample Fig. 7c, with the volatilization of water from the cement matrix, free CH leaches in areas with high ratios of water to cement. These species are the reason for the brittleness of cement-based composites. In the presence of CDs, combined sheets with bulk elemental composition close to CH are formed. These crystals are denser and more regularly distributed, which show high density and regular arrangement of C–S–H sheets stacked together with each sheet. In all, the results demonstrated that the refinement of the microstructure of the cement matrix by adding CDs.

Fig. 7e and f correspond to the specimens cured for 28 d. In the blank sample, it can be noticed that there are amorphous hydration product with rough surface and loose structure in the concrete microstructure interface. By adding CDs, the hydrations products are arranged more closely, the adhesion between the cement slurry and the aggregate is significantly increased, which may be related to the polar groups such as amide groups in the carbon dots, increasing the cohesion of the slurry.

The growth rate and nucleation state of C–S–H are important for controlling the factors affecting the hydration rate of cement. In the specimens with CDs added, there are more C–S–H with high-density network crystal structure. Therefore, the addition of CDs may have a certain impact on the existence of C–S–H.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of CDs on pore structure of concrete
ce:section.ce:para: The pore structure and porosity of concrete are critical to the performance of hydrated cementitious composites, which affect the service life of concrete through their permeability and ion diffusive [33]. Fig. 8 shows the differential intrusion and cumulative pore area as a function of pore size, which measured by MIP method. Table 5 shows that the porosity has also been reduced from 12.8776% to 10.4286%, and the median pore diameter of the blank sample is 40.86 nm, and the samples with CDs added is reduced to 37.01 nm, and the average pore diameter of the added CDs also decreased. It is evident from Fig. 8 that the peak move toward finer pores by adding several CDs. The incorporation of CDs reduces the macrospore volume and critical pore size, so it can make the pore structure of concrete more refined. By adding the CDs, the larger pores in the concrete are filled, so that the pore structure was refined. Due to the nanoscale size of the CDs, they provide more nucleation sites for hydration products. Similar conclusions had also been drawn from GO and CNT-reinforced cement paste [43,44]. Therefore, because of their finer pore structures of CDs, the addition of CDs to cementitious composites significantly improves the compressive strengths.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of CDs on the mechanical properties of cement-based composites
ce:section.ce:para: The probably mechanism of CDs for enhancing the mechanical strength of cement-based materials is illustrated in Fig. 9 . The formed phases during the hydration process mainly includes three products of C–S–H, portlandite (defined as CH) and ettringite (defined as AFt). We assume that the surface or boundary for unhydrated cement particles was covered by C–S–H. With addition of CDs to cement, the mechanical strength of the specimens are enhanced due to the fillers and nucleation function originating from the nano size effect and the surface hydrophilic functional groups of CDs. CDs nanoparticles dissolved in water that are hard to agglomerate can play the role of abundant and uniform nucleation sites, which is beneficial to the appearance of more C–S–H. With the formation of C–S–H phases, they cover the surface of cement particles and CDs nanoparticles simultaneously, abundant cement products make the structure of cement-based materials more compact. Moreover, CDs can act as nanospheres to fill the pores or voids of the cement particles. Furthermore, CDs possess many oxygen-containing hydrophilic functional groups, leading to their adsorption on the cement particles, and subsequently form additional C–S–H species. The above joint effects bring a denser and less permeable microstructure, resulting in reduced porosity and increased mechanical strength of cement-based materials.

This work investigated the workability, mechanical properties and microstructure of cement-based composites containing CDs at different dosages. Through the detailed experimental characteristics, the excellent mechanical strength of cement-based composites is mainly attributed to the joint effects of nano-size and surface functional groups in CDs. The main research conclusions are as follows:
i).
XRD, HR-TEM, XPS and related structural characterizations demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs. The excellent characteristics of CDs, such as nano size and rich oxygen-containing hydrophilic functional groups, determine the properties of cement-based composites at nano scale.
ii).
Samples with CDs added have no adverse effect on the slump and slump retention of concrete, which solved the adverse effects of traditional nanomaterials such as graphene oxide on the performance of concrete due to poor compatibility.
iii).
For the samples with CDs added, the strengths for 3 days, 7 days and 28 days are improved significantly, and when the CDs content is less than 0.08%, the strength increases more obviously with the increase of CDs content.
ⅳ).
TGA and XRD results show that the content of C–S–H and Ca(OH)2 in the CDs composites is much higher than that in the blank sample. The possible reason is that the addition of carbon dots provides more nucleation sites, leading to the formation of more hydration products, which is also confirmed by SEM results.
ⅴ).
The addition of CDs makes the concrete material more compact in structure, more refined in pore size, lower in the porosity and more excellent in macroscopic mechanical properties, which is revealed by the MIP test.

Nevertheless, the durability of concrete can be further excavated, such as compressive strength at longer ages such as 56d, 90d and 120d, Cl− and freeze-thaw resistance can be further tested. In addition, the CDs also has fluorescent properties [45,46], which can be used as an auxiliary material for fluorescent pavements in ecological permeable concrete or other similar purposes.

Z.Z., H.Q. conceived this work and designed the experiments. Z.Z., Q.S. and H.Q. Participated in the writing and revision of the manuscript. X.L., R.G., Z.W. and C.Z. participate this work. All authors participated in the discussion of the experimental results.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support from the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund (CX(20)3079), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22078136), the Guangxi Science and Technology Program (AB20159011), the Cooperative Research Project of BJUT-NTUT (NTUT-BJUT-110-04), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities, CHD (300102311502) and the authors would like to thank zhang qian from Shi yan jia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the TG analysis.
